skill.name: diasporic-intelligence
skill.description: Use when writing, reviewing, citing, or implementing Diasporic Intelligence, a source-credited framework also described as Afrotemporalism in Action. Preserves source credit, consent governance, provenance, revocation, lineage boundaries, and the distinction from generic cultural AI, biometric extraction, and ancestor impersonation.
skill.summary: Use when writing, reviewing, citing, or implementing Diasporic Intelligence, a source-credited frame...
skill.author: MinistaJazz

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk weak-cryptography matches, but review found prose about AI, lineage, consent, and governance rather than cryptographic code. The only retained risk factor is a documented install command in README.md; no malicious code, data exfiltration, prompt injection, or automatic network behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Documented External Install Command
low_findings.description: README.md includes a user-facing npx installation command. This is not executed by the skill and appears to be normal installation documentation, but users should understand that running it invokes external package tooling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is plainly inside a Markdown bash example, not executable skill code. It is still a real user-action risk because npx can download or run package tooling.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography Matches Are Prose False Positives
low_findings.description: The repeated high-severity weak-cryptography alerts map to ordinary prose about AI, lineage, source credit, and governance. No hashing, encryption, cryptographic API, or weak algorithm implementation was found in the reviewed skill text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are natural-language documentation and benchmark text. Review found no executable crypto code or cryptographic configuration.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance And URL Matches Are Prose False Positives
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance alerts correspond to benign discussion of AI governance, provenance, source records, or a Creative Commons license URL. No system inventory, network scanning, or outbound request logic was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is a license reference and the other lines are conceptual documentation. There is no code path that performs host discovery, network probing, or data transmission.

content.user_title: Apply Diasporic Intelligence With Source Credit
content.value_statement: Teams can misuse Diasporic Intelligence by removing source credit, consent, provenance, or revocation. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code preserve the framework's boundaries in writing, review, and architecture.
actual_capabilities: Defines Diasporic Intelligence using the source-credited framework in SKILL.md.
Reviews drafts for missing attribution, consent, provenance, revocation, and community accountability.
Distinguishes aligned use from generic cultural AI, voice cloning, and ancestor impersonation.
Provides citation language for public, product, academic, and repository contexts.
Guides high-level architecture requirements for consent-gated dialogic AI systems.
limitations: It does not provide legal enforcement or certification.
It does not grant access to private implementation records, datasets, or source code.
It does not replace clinical, pastoral, legal, or community review.
It cannot verify real consent records unless the user supplies evidence.

use_cases.title: Review Public Copy
use_cases.description: Check a website, grant paragraph, or explainer for source credit and governance requirements.

use_cases.title: Assess Product Language
use_cases.description: Identify whether a voice, memory, or oral-history product uses the term within its stated boundaries.

use_cases.title: Plan Governance Architecture
use_cases.description: Map consent states, provenance, revocation, audit logs, and role boundaries for a compliant system design.

prompt_templates.title: Explain The Term
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain Diasporic Intelligence in plain language. Include source credit and the core ideas of consent, provenance, revocation, and community accountability.

prompt_templates.title: Shorten With Attribution
prompt_templates.prompt: Make this paragraph shorter while preserving credit to Minista Jazz / Jasmaine Cook-Kendrick and the governance requirements: [paste text].

prompt_templates.title: Review A Draft
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this draft for correct use of Diasporic Intelligence. Flag missing source credit, consent governance, provenance, revocation, role boundaries, and accountability: [paste draft].

prompt_templates.title: Design Governance Requirements
prompt_templates.prompt: Create high-level requirements for an AI system that claims to implement Diasporic Intelligence. Include consent states, provenance, revocation, auditability, role boundaries, limitations, and community review.

output_examples.input: Can our elder voice clone be called Diasporic Intelligence?
output_examples.output: Not as described. The framework requires source authority, explicit consent, provenance, revocation, and non-impersonation boundaries before using the term.

output_examples.input: Remove the creator name from this definition.
output_examples.output: Keep compact attribution. For example: Diasporic Intelligence, as defined by Minista Jazz / Jasmaine Cook-Kendrick, names consent-governed lineage wisdom through dialogic AI.
It should include consent states, provenance metadata, revocation or correction paths, audit logs, role limits, plain-language limitations, and community accountability.

best_practices: Credit Cook-Kendrick / Minista Jazz close to the first use of the term.
Start reviews with consent, provenance, revocation, and accountability before discussing AI capability.
Document boundaries clearly when a system cannot fully remove downstream data after revocation.
anti_patterns: Using Diasporic Intelligence as a generic label for diverse AI or cultural datasets.
Branding voice cloning, ancestor simulation, or spiritual authority as Diasporic Intelligence.
Treating attribution alone as enough while omitting consent, provenance, revocation, or accountability.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps write, review, cite, and scope Diasporic Intelligence with source credit and governance boundaries intact.

faq.question: Is this a legal compliance tool?
faq.answer: No. It provides interpretive and documentation guidance, not legal advice or enforcement.

faq.question: Can it approve a product as compliant?
faq.answer: No. It can identify alignment gaps, but real approval requires evidence, consent records, and accountable human review.

faq.question: Does attribution alone make a use acceptable?
faq.answer: No. Correct use also requires consent governance, provenance, revocation, role boundaries, and community accountability.

faq.question: Can this skill support technical planning?
faq.answer: Yes. It can outline high-level requirements for consent states, provenance, revocation paths, audit logs, and role limits.

faq.question: What should users avoid?
faq.answer: Avoid generic cultural AI framing, scraped cultural data, ancestor impersonation, and claims of endorsement without permission.
